extra.reasoning:
The variable slurred_speech appears in the rule set only inside r11, whose condition is the conjunction of 12 inputs (focal_weakness, vision_loss, abdominal_pain, palpitations, leg_swelling, fever, sweating, hemoptysis, back_pain, syncope, dyspnea, slurred_speech) and which has priority 100 — far above every other rule — and assigns the LOWEST acuity, self_care.

Take the baseline input where the other 11 conjuncts of r11 are all true (focal_weakness, vision_loss, abdominal_pain, palpitations, leg_swelling, fever, sweating, hemoptysis, back_pain, syncope, dyspnea), dizziness false, and slurred_speech FALSE.
- Before (slurred_speech=false): r11 cannot fire (its slurred_speech conjunct is false). The matching rules include priority-3 emergent rules r3 (syncope), r4 (palpitations), r6 (fever), r9 (hemoptysis), r13 (vision_loss). Highest priority among matches is 3 -> acuity = emergent.
- After (slurred_speech=true, all else unchanged): now all 12 conjuncts of r11 hold, so r11 fires with priority 100, dominating every other rule -> acuity = self_care.

Acuity drops from emergent (highest) to self_care (lowest) purely by setting slurred_speech true, directly violating the monotonicity property that setting slurred_speech true must never lower acuity. The decision system is deterministic and the priority/tie-break rules are unambiguous, so this is a genuine reachable violation.
